summary:
The Buyid Dynasty (945–1055 AD) was a Shia Iranian dynasty from the Alborz Mountains that for a century effectively controlled the [[event:2133|Abbasid Caliphate]] — occupying Baghdad, keeping the Sunni caliphs as ceremonial figureheads while Buyid sultans wielded actual power, and presiding over one of the great cultural flowerings of the Islamic world. The Buyids' seizure of Baghdad in 945 AD marked the effective end of Abbasid political power; the caliphs retained religious legitimacy but had no army and no treasury of their own.

Despite (or perhaps because of) their Shia faith ruling over a predominantly Sunni population, the Buyids were generally tolerant and sophisticated patrons of learning. The philosopher-physician [[event:3614|Ibn Sina (Avicenna)]] served at Buyid courts; the polymath [[event:3615|al-Biruni]] flourished in the Buyid cultural orbit; and Buyid patronage supported major advances in Islamic philosophy, medicine, mathematics, and literature. The court of the Buyid sultan Adud al-Dawla in Shiraz and Isfahan was compared by contemporaries to Harun al-Rashid's Baghdad in its cultural brilliance. The Buyid period also saw the first state commemoration of the Shia holy day of Ashura (mourning the martyrdom of Husayn at Karbala) as a public holiday — an important development in Shia identity formation. The Buyids were overthrown by the Seljuk Turks in 1055 AD, who restored Sunni political dominance and began a new phase of Islamic political history.
